abstract: James M. Bell and Zeek Starr provide supplemental testimony on behalf of the applicants in the Parmelia Rowe et al. case for enrollment as Cherokee Freedmen. James M. Bell of Delaware District testifies that he knew "Aunt" Sallie Mayfield, the enslaver of Parmelia Rowe, in the Cherokee Nation before she moved to Texas in 1847, and that his father had moved to Rusk County, Texas, during the same year. He testifies that he did not know Parmelia Rowe or the names of any of the people who were enslaved by Sallie Mayfield. He testifies knowing Jess Rowe, who was formerly enslaved by George Starr and also came to Texas in 1847. Bell claims that Sallie Mayfield, Jess Rowe, and his father were all "Cherokee by blood" and have continuously resided in Texas. Zeek Starr, the son of George Starr, testifies that he was born in Texas in 1849, after his family had moved there, and that Jess, one of their enslaved persons, lived with them  there during the Civil War. He also testifies that he knew "Aunt" Sallie Mayfield in Texas and that his father was re-admitted as a Cherokee citizen after returning to the Cherokee Nation after the war.

abstract: In this letter, A.C. Tonner,  the Acting Commissioner of the Office of Indian Affairs presents a report from the Commission to the Five Civilized Tribes to the U.S. Secretary of the Interior, dated March 15, 1904. He describes the reasons behind the Commission's decision to reject the Cherokee Freedmen application for Jesse Hicks and his son (William Hicks"). It describes how Hicks, who was formerly enslaved by a Cherokee citizen, left the Cherokee Nation during the Civil War, returning to it in 1867, only to relocate to Kansas for the next twenty ears.  In the meantime, his son, born in 1887, is declared to have "no right as a Cherokee Freedman except as derived from his father."  The letter ends with a recommendation of this decision.

abstract: Testimony of Ruth Hunt, a Cherokee Freedman from Vinita on behalf of Martha Albert and her children for membership in the Cherokee Nation. Ruth testifies that she spent the Civil War in Fort Smith, Arkansas, and that Martha's husband worked for her family in 1867 after she returned to the Cherokee Nation. Also includes the testimonies of Emma Finley, the sister of Ruth Hunt, from Tahlequah Territory, and Harry Still, a Cherokee Freedman and former enslaved person. According to his testimony, Harry Still had been previously enslaved by George Whitmire, the brother of Johnson Whitmire, Martha Albert's former enslaver.

abstract: Testimony of Lula Knalls on behalf of herself and her three children for enrollment as Cherokee Freedmen. Martha Albert, her aunt, also serves as a witness during this hearing. Lula is the daughter of "Jenny Barnes" and the half-sister of Robert Barnes. Although Lula claims to have lived in the Cherokee Nation (on "Lee's Creek" and in the Sequoyah District) during her entire life, she cannot be identified on the census rolls of 1880 or 1896, or on the Kerns-Clifton roll. The decision is made to list Lula, as well as her daughter Sarah French and her son, Fred E. Knalls, on a "doubtful card" for further consideration. Meanwhile, her oldest son, Frank Gardner, who was twenty-two years old at the time, would be required to apply separately on his own behalf.

abstract: The official decision of the Commission to the Five Civilized Tribes to deny the application of John May and his children (Sophia, Ettie, Minnie, Elmer and Idella May) for enrollment as Cherokee Freedmen. It states that he may have appeared before the Commission on June 24, 1901 in Nowata, Indian Territory in order to testify.  The evidence shows that he was formerly enslaved by a Cherokee citizen and left the Cherokee Nation during the Civil War, but did not return within the required timeframe, as decreed in the case of "Moses Whitmire, trustee, etc. vs. the Cherokee Nation et al." in 1896. None of the applicants were identified in the "authenticated" 1880 Cherokee tribal roll. It was decided to deny this case according to provisions in the Curtis Act, approved by Congress on June 28, 1898.

abstract: Letter from Tams Bixby, the Commissioner of the Five Civilized Tribes to Henry Albert of Vian Indian Territory. The letter informs him of the denial of his Cherokee Freedmen application for himself and his children, Harry, Raymond, and Lewey Albert. It also informs him that a copy of the Commissioner's decision has been sent to his attorneys, Neal and London of Fort Smith, Arkansas.
